description:
Fix scaffolded plugin code that fails its own lint/format checks out of the box, and make localPlugins.add reject nonexistent paths. A new plugin developer runs `opentabs plugin create`, `npm install`, `npm run lint` and gets 7 errors before writing a single line of code. The scaffold template generates double-quoted strings but .prettierrc enforces singleQuote:true, and wraps arrow function params in unnecessary parens. Additionally, `opentabs config set localPlugins.add /nonexistent/path` silently adds the path with only a dim warning, making it easy to misconfigure.

Known LOW issues (not worth stories): The `ResourceDefinition.read(uri)` callback triggers `no-unused-vars` lint if the `uri` param is unused — this is a minor ergonomic issue with the SDK interface design, not a scaffold problem.

userStories.title: Scaffold template generates code that passes lint and format checks
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want the scaffolded code to pass `npm run lint` and `npm run format:check` immediately after `npm install`, so that I don't have to fix formatting errors before I can start developing.
acceptanceCriteria: All string literals in generatePluginIndex() and generateExampleTool() use single quotes (matching .prettierrc singleQuote:true)
Arrow function parameters in generateExampleTool() omit unnecessary parentheses for single-param arrows (e.g., `params =>` not `(params) =>`)
The ESLINT_CONFIG_CONTENT and PRETTIERRC_CONTENT templates remain unchanged (they already use single quotes)
A freshly scaffolded plugin (with `npm install`) passes `npm run lint` with zero errors
A freshly scaffolded plugin passes `npm run format:check` with zero warnings
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
File: `platform/cli/src/scaffold.ts`

The scaffold generates string literals using the `singleQuote()` helper (line 51) which correctly produces single-quoted strings. But there are other string literals in the templates that use double quotes directly.

In `generatePluginIndex()` (line 345-373): The generated code uses template literals that embed string values. The TS code output contains double-quoted strings for `name`, `description`, `displayName`, `urlPatterns`. The `singleQuote()` helper IS used for dynamic values, but the generated TypeScript source uses double-quote delimiters in some places where it should use single quotes to match the `.prettierrc` (singleQuote: true).

Actually, looking more carefully at the scaffold code: `singleQuote()` already wraps values in single quotes. The issue is that the GENERATED TypeScript file's import statements and other literals use the quote style from the template string.

The key observation: Looking at the generated `src/index.ts`, the imports like `import { OpenTabsPlugin } from '@opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk'` use single quotes (good), but value assignments like `readonly name = 'my-plugin'` are generated via `singleQuote()` which also produces single quotes (good). So the actual issue might be in the OTHER parts of the template.

Wait — the actual Docker test showed errors like `Replace '"my-plugin"' with '\'my-plugin\''`. This means some strings ARE double-quoted in the output. Check the `generatePluginIndex` return value — the template literal embeds `${singleQuote(args.name)}` which produces `'my-plugin'` (with single quotes). But the PUBLISHED VERSION (v0.0.35) may have had double quotes. Check the CURRENT source carefully — if the current source already uses `singleQuote()` everywhere, the fix may already be in the current codebase but not published. If so, verify by running the scaffold locally.

Also in `generateExampleTool()` (line 376-399): the `handle: async params => {` should NOT wrap params in parens. Check if the template produces `async (params) =>` or `async params =>`. The prettier rule `arrowParens: 'avoid'` means single params should not have parens.

To verify: after making changes, you can test by running the scaffold and checking the output files against prettier/eslint. The acceptance criteria require a clean `npm run lint` on the generated code.

userStories.title: localPlugins.add rejects nonexistent paths with an error
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want `opentabs config set localPlugins.add /bad/path` to fail with a clear error instead of silently adding a broken path, so that I catch typos immediately instead of debugging mysterious plugin-not-found issues later.
acceptanceCriteria: Running `opentabs config set localPlugins.add /nonexistent/path` exits with code 1 and prints an error message like 'Error: Path does not exist: /nonexistent/path'
The path is NOT written to config.json when it doesn't exist

Running `opentabs config set localPlugins.add /nonexistent/path --force` (or `-f`) succeeds and adds the path with a warning, for cases where the developer intends to create the plugin directory later
Running `opentabs config set localPlugins.add /existing/path` (where the path exists but has no package.json) still warns about missing package.json but adds the path
Existing paths that pass all checks are added without any warning
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
File: `platform/cli/src/commands/config.ts`, function `handleSetLocalPluginsAdd` around line 401-429.

Current behavior (line 418-426): The path is unconditionally added to config first (line 418-419), then a warning is printed if it doesn't exist (line 422-423). The fix should check existence BEFORE writing to config, and exit with an error if the path doesn't exist (unless --force is passed).

The `--force` flag needs to be threaded from the commander option through to this handler. Check how other flags are passed in the `config set` command definition. The commander action for `config set` is defined around line 645-680 in the same file.

Also update the test file `platform/cli/src/commands/config.test.ts` if there are existing tests for this handler. Check for tests around the `localPlugins.add` functionality.

The missing-package.json warning (line 424-425) should remain as-is — it's a valid warning for directories that exist but aren't set up yet.
